explanation: This statement says that one of prop_0, prop_1, or prop_2 must occur before prop_3 happens. In LTL, we use 'until' to express that prop_3 does not occur until one of the three properties has occurred. So: (!prop_3) U (prop_0 | prop_1 | prop_2).

The phrase means that before prop_3 happens, at least one of prop_0, prop_1, or prop_2 must occur. This can be expressed as: 'It is always the case that until prop_3 happens, at least one of prop_0, prop_1, or prop_2 must eventually occur.'

Since only 'prop_0', 'prop_1', and 'prop_2' are valid, the requirement reduces to: once either prop_0 or prop_1 occurs, and this must happen before prop_2. So, before the first occurrence of prop_2, at least one of prop_0 or prop_1 must happen.

The property expresses that at some point before 'prop_3' becomes true, one of 'prop_0', 'prop_1', or 'prop_2' must happen (i.e., become true at least once). This can be captured by 'not prop_3' holding until ('prop_0' or 'prop_1' or 'prop_2') holds. If 'prop_3' never happens, then it's simply required that one of those propositions eventually occurs. In LTL, '(not prop_3) U (prop_0 | prop_1 | prop_2)' requires that before 'prop_3' can occur, at least one of the stated propositions must occur.

This means that once prop_0 becomes true, it is required that before prop_3 occurs, either prop_1 or prop_2 must occur. In other words, globally, whenever prop_0 occurs, then it must be the case that (prop_1 or prop_2) occurs before the next occurrence of prop_3. In LTL, this is usually formalized by: G(prop_0 -> ((!(prop_3)) U (prop_1 | prop_2) ) ). But to capture the 'before the prop_3', we want to ensure (prop_1 or prop_2) happens before prop_3 after prop_0.

The statement asks that at some point before 'prop_3' holds, at least one of 'prop_0', 'prop_1', or 'prop_2' must occur (possibly simultaneously). This is typically formalized as (¬prop_3) U ((prop_0 | prop_1 | prop_2) & ¬prop_3): 'before prop_3', one of the others must occur. Alternatively, 'eventually before prop_3' (i.e., as long as you haven't seen prop_3 yet, you will find prop_0 or prop_1 or prop_2).

The phrase 'Once the prop_0 the prop_1 or prop_2 before the prop_3' seems to mean that at some point before (but not including) prop_3, either prop_1 or prop_2 must hold, and this must be preceded by prop_0 holding. So, the interpretation is: before prop_3, after seeing prop_0, prop_1 or prop_2 must occur (at least once). This can be expressed as: globally, if prop_0 holds before prop_3, then eventually (before prop_3), prop_1 or prop_2 must occur.

This statement says that prop_0, or prop_1, or prop_2 must occur at least once before the very first time prop_3 becomes true. In LTL, this is commonly expressed by stating that, until prop_3 occurs, it is always the case that eventually (in the future) at least one of prop_0, prop_1, or prop_2 will occur. Alternatively, you can require that if prop_3 has not yet happened, eventually one of those will occur before it. This is captured by: before prop_3, eventually (prop_0 | prop_1 | prop_2) happens.

We interpret this as: 'prop_0' must happen once, and it must happen before either 'prop_1' or 'prop_2' occurs, and all of these must happen before 'prop_3'. So, 'prop_0' occurs at some point before (prop_1 or prop_2), and both 'prop_0' and ('prop_1' or 'prop_2') occur before 'prop_3'.

The statement means that at some point, either prop_1 or prop_2 must occur before prop_3 does, and after that, prop_0 must eventually occur. In LTL, 'p before q' is often expressed by '(!q) U (p)', meaning p happens before q. Here, it is 'prop_1 or prop_2' before 'prop_3', and after that, 'prop_0' must eventually happen. Given the statement begins 'once the prop_0', it can be interpreted as: It is always the case that if 'prop_3' occurs, then ('prop_1 or prop_2') has already occurred before it, and eventually prop_0 also occurs.
